text: The robotic system is docked for revision of the iliorectal anastomosis. The surgeon observes a distended small bowel upon entering the abdomen. Using a robotic vessel sealer, the rectum is incised three centimeters distal to the iliorectal anastomosis and circumferentially transected. A small contained abscess is discovered near the posterior wall, indicating an anastomotic leak. The surgeon then incises the ileum two centimeters proximal to the iliorectal anastomosis until it is completely transected.

The surgeon cuts the specimen into smaller pieces for trans-anal extraction using a natural orifice specimen extraction technique. The specimen is removed trans-anally with a sponge clamp. Endocyanin green is injected to assess bowel viability at the distal rectal margin and proximal ileal margin, showing good perfusion at both sites.

The surgeon performs a hand-sewn anastomosis between the ileum and rectum using three visible barbed sutures in a running fashion. The posterior wall of the anastomosis is completed with a simple running suture technique.

The surgeon performs a running canal stitch on the anterior wall of the anastomosis. After completing the anastomosis, an air leak test is conducted using flexible sigmoidoscopy, air insufflation, and submerging the anastomosis in normal saline. The endoscopic inspection reveals a patent and hemostatic anastomosis, with no bubbling observed during the air leak test, indicating an airtight seal. The decision is made to forego diverting loop ileostomy due to the patient's obesity and the confined nature of the abscess. Pathological analysis of the resected segment shows extensive necrosis, cirrhositis, and inflammation, but no malignancy.
